docs.name: STRAIN Z
docs.author: STRAIN Z
docs.summary: A slow-burn survival-horror system. Roll 3d6 and try to roll equal to or under your Attribute plus an Ease Modifier. Lethal, fast, and built on dread: the cold, fuel, panic, and other living people are the real threats. The dead are the pressure that turns ordinary problems deadly.

dice.summary: Roll 3d6 ≤ Attribute + Ease. Natural 3 = crit success, natural 18 = crit failure. Beat the target by 5+ for a Clean Hit (+2 damage). To hit, the target's Defense is your Ease; on a hit, damage − Soak comes off VIT (or a zombie's Body — only a destroyed head is a true kill).

attributes.name: Brawn
attributes.desc: Melee, lifting, endurance, resisting cold & illness, soaking damage.

attributes.name: Agility
attributes.desc: Ranged attacks, driving, climbing, stealth, reflexes, fine motor work.

attributes.name: Wits
attributes.desc: Perception, mechanics, medicine, scavenging, planning, holding your nerve.

attributes.name: Charm
attributes.desc: Persuasion, lying, leadership, reading people, calming panic.

deriveds.name: Vitality
deriveds.desc: Physical buffer before you go Down. Down at 0. Lost to wounds (damage − Soak); recovered with rest, warmth, food, and care.

deriveds.name: Composure
deriveds.desc: Your nerve under horror. Break at 0. Spent by gore, panic, and impossible choices; recovered by safety, warmth, and a steadying word.

deriveds.name: Defense
deriveds.desc: How hard you are to hit. An attacker rolls at an Ease penalty equal to your Defense; cover and footing raise it, and being Down, grabbed, or surrounded drops it (often to −2 or worse). +2 ≈ Hard to hit.

easeLadder.name: Simple
easeLadder.use: Favorable conditions, good tools, no pressure

easeLadder.name: Hard
easeLadder.use: Bad footing, poor light, improvised tools, real danger

easeLadder.name: Desperate
easeLadder.use: Long odds, terrible conditions, doing it while fighting

tracks.name: Cold & Exposure
tracks.desc: Hourly Brawn check, difficulty worsening one step each consecutive exposed hour. Warmth halts and reverses it (recover one stage per ~30 min).

stages.name: Shivering
stages.effect: −2 to all rolls; fine-motor tasks one step harder.

stages.name: Frostnipped
stages.effect: −4 to all rolls; hand tasks at Desperate; risk losing fingers/toes.

stages.name: Hypothermic
stages.effect: Incapacitated, drifting under. Without warming, die in ~1 hour — then turn.

conditions.name: Shaking Hands
conditions.effect: −2 to your next action (fear, cold, adrenaline).

conditions.name: Tunnel Vision
conditions.effect: Can't perceive or act on anything outside your fixation until something snaps you out.

conditions.name: Rooted
conditions.effect: Can't move from your spot for a round (fear-locked or pinned).

conditions.name: Grabbed
conditions.effect: A foe has hold of you. −2 Defense and you can't move until you break free (Desperate Brawn or Agility). Held by the dead = a bite each round.

conditions.name: Prone
conditions.effect: Knocked down. −2 to act; attackers in reach are at +2 (Simple). Standing is your action.

conditions.name: Deafened / Half-Deaf
conditions.effect: Muffled hearing (heavy helmet, a gunshot, ringing ears). −2 to notice or locate threats by sound — and the dead hunt by sound.

conditions.name: Bitten
conditions.effect: Skin broken by the dead — you die in 1d6 days, then turn. No cure. (Amputation within a round or two is a desperate, maiming gamble some try.)

conditions.name: Bleeding Out (Down)
conditions.effect: At 0 VIT. Dies in 1d6 rounds (in combat) or 1d6×10 minutes (after) without aid.

conditions.name: Injured (Minor / Major / Grievous)
conditions.effect: A lasting wound. Minor: −1 to related rolls. Major: −2 and a clear limitation (limp, bad arm). Grievous: −4 and barely functional. Heal one tier per few days of real rest and care.

conditions.name: Broken (0 Composure)
conditions.effect: Freeze, Flight, Fixate, or Lash Out (roll or choose) until someone steadies you (Charm) or the threat ends.

conditions.name: Burdened
conditions.effect: Carrying too much. −1 Agility (and Defense), louder, and you tire faster. Drop the bag to run.

clockTemplates.name: The Highway Wakes
clockTemplates.desc: Fills as people die and as the players make noise. When full, the slaughter becomes general.

clockTemplates.name: Someone Dies Inside
clockTemplates.desc: The crowded ark / safe-zone clock. When full, the turn begins in a locked, crowded room.

clockTemplates.name: The Horde Turns
clockTemplates.desc: Fills with every noise and misstep. When full, the crowd floods toward you as one.

clockTemplates.name: Across
clockTemplates.desc: Progress through a gauntlet (e.g. the Whiskey Row alleys). Fill it before the Horde turns.

clockTemplates.name: Fuel
clockTemplates.desc: The SUV's tank in quarters. Idling with the heater burns ~a quarter per 2–3 hours.

backgrounds.name: Ranch Hand
backgrounds.gear: Work gloves, multi-tool, length of rope, lighter

backgrounds.name: Long-Haul Trucker
backgrounds.gear: CB radio knowledge, tire iron, thermos, road atlas

backgrounds.name: Hunter
backgrounds.gear: Hunting rifle (if allowed) or the eye for one, knife, jerky

backgrounds.name: Mechanic
backgrounds.gear: Toolbox, flashlight, siphon hose, shop rag, half-tank fuel can

backgrounds.name: Schoolteacher
backgrounds.gear: First-aid basics, granola bars, reroll one Composure check/session

backgrounds.name: Bartender
backgrounds.gear: Charm Knack on de-escalation, a flask, surprising toughness

backgrounds.name: College Athlete
backgrounds.gear: Good shoes (rare!), water bottle, raw Agility

backgrounds.name: Retired Vet (Animal or Army)
backgrounds.knack: GM's choice: field medicine or steady hands
backgrounds.gear: A useful old habit and a calm under fire

weapons.name: Fists / kick
weapons.notes: Bad idea vs the dead — your hand is in bite range. Fine for living scuffles.

weapons.name: Improvised (branch, bottle, lamp)
weapons.notes: Whatever's to hand. May break on a Critical Failure.

weapons.name: Knife / hatchet
weapons.notes: Concealable and silent; ideal for a finishing headshot at arm's reach.

weapons.name: Machete / cleaver
weapons.notes: Cuts deep; can sever a grabbing arm on a Clean Hit.

weapons.name: Bat / crowbar / tire iron
weapons.notes: Sturdy, reusable, silent. A crowbar also pries doors and lids.

weapons.name: Spear / improvised polearm
weapons.notes: Reach lets you strike first and keep bites at arm's length — the smart melee against the dead.

weapons.name: Fire axe / sledge
weapons.notes: Heavy and slow; loud impacts. A Clean Hit splits a skull outright.

weapons.name: Chainsaw
weapons.notes: Terrifying and brutal — and a dinner bell. +2 to the nearest Horde clock every round it runs; burns fuel.

weapons.name: Bow / crossbow
weapons.notes: The only truly quiet ranged option, and you can recover arrows/bolts. Crossbow hits harder but reloads slowly.

weapons.name: Nail gun
weapons.notes: Close, quiet, weak; needs gas or a compressor/power.

weapons.name: .22 rifle
weapons.notes: A light crack rather than a boom; weak but plenty for a headshot, and the ammo is everywhere. +0 or +1 Horde clock at the GM's call.

weapons.name: Pistol
weapons.notes: Loud. +1 to the nearest Horde clock per shot.

weapons.name: Revolver
weapons.notes: Reliable — it never jams — but slow to reload, round by round.

weapons.name: Shotgun
weapons.notes: Devastating up close; the spread helps against a lunging dead. Deafeningly loud — +2 Horde clock.

weapons.name: Hunting rifle
weapons.notes: The best tool for ranged headshots. Bolt-action is slow; one careful shot at a time. +1 Horde clock.

weapons.name: Molotov / fire
weapons.notes: Lights an area or a chokepoint; anything in it takes mounting fire damage. Fire draws every dead thing for blocks — and doesn't care whose building it's in.

weapons.name: Bear spray / taser
weapons.notes: Useless on the dead (no pain, no breath). Buys precious seconds against a living threat.

vehicles.name: Family SUV (theirs)
vehicles.notes: Warm, thirsty, useless in deep mud.

vehicles.name: Lifted pickup
vehicles.notes: Tempting for the median — until it sinks.

vehicles.name: ADOT Snowplow
vehicles.notes: Chains, weight, a blade that shoves cars. The campaign's golden ticket.

vehicles.name: Sedan / minivan
vehicles.notes: Plentiful, low on fuel, often with grim contents.

bestiary.name: Wakers (Freshly Turned)
bestiary.bite: Any clean hit breaks skin → start an infection clock. A grab needs a Desperate Brawn/Agility check to shake.
bestiary.drive: Chase, climb, vault, even work a door handle for a while.
bestiary.tactics: They run you down and swarm. Don't try to out-sprint them in the open; use doors, height, and chokepoints.
bestiary.notes: Recently dead, intact, fast, eerily coordinated — they remember how to move. This is what makes the first hour terrifying.

bestiary.name: Shamblers
bestiary.bite: Bite on a hit → infection clock.
bestiary.drive: Drift toward sound and movement; mob anything cornered.
bestiary.tactics: Individually manageable; deadly in numbers, in the dark, or when they pin you. Keep moving and keep your back clear.
bestiary.notes: The standard dead after stiffening sets in. The bulk of any crowd.

bestiary.name: Cold-Stiffs
bestiary.drive: Barely move — until warmth wakes them.
bestiary.tactics: Easy to behead while frozen. The trap is leading them somewhere warm: near a fire or a running engine they thaw and reactivate.
bestiary.notes: Walkers seized by deep cold. A gift and a trap.

bestiary.name: The Trapped
bestiary.bite: Lunges and bites anything within reach of the wreck/belt.
bestiary.drive: Strain toward you from where they're pinned.
bestiary.tactics: Can't pursue — but reaching past them is how people get bitten. Whole rows of I-17 cars become this overnight.
bestiary.kill: Destroy the head; or simply don't put a limb in reach.
bestiary.notes: Pinned in seatbelts, wreckage, collapsed structures.

bestiary.name: Crawlers
bestiary.bite: Ankle-grab from below → you're Grabbed and likely Prone, then bitten.
bestiary.drive: Drag themselves toward warmth and movement.
bestiary.tactics: Slow but invisible in snow, under cars, in tall grass. Watch your feet in the dark.
bestiary.notes: Any walker reduced below the waist. The ankle-grab in the dark.

bestiary.name: Screamer (Howler)
bestiary.drive: Wails constantly — a living siren for the dead.
bestiary.tactics: Every round it's loose, +1 to the nearest Horde/Wakes clock. Silence it first, even at a risk — or leave the area fast.
bestiary.notes: A turned throat stuck mid-scream. The thing that turns one bad room into a horde.

bestiary.name: Bloater (Gas-Bloat)
bestiary.bite: Weak bite; the danger is the burst.
bestiary.tactics: Fragile — but killing it up close (blunt/fire) bursts it: a choking, infectious cloud (everyone adjacent rolls Brawn or acts one step harder for a round; spatter counts as a skin-break risk). Pop it at range.
bestiary.kill: Destroy the head — ideally from a few steps back.
bestiary.notes: Days of bloat under the snow. A trap dressed as an easy kill.

bestiary.name: Pack (Coordinated Fresh)
bestiary.drive: Hunt together, cut off exits, herd prey.
bestiary.tactics: Run a small group of fresh dead as a 4–6 step Clock plus a couple of individuals; each tick they close a route. They punish anyone who runs blind.
bestiary.kill: Destroy heads; better, don't let them surround you.
bestiary.notes: 3–6 Wakers that move like they remember being a crew.

bestiary.name: The Horde
bestiary.bite: When the Clock fills, everyone in reach makes a Desperate Agility check to break free or starts taking bites.
bestiary.drive: Flow toward noise, light, and fire as one slow tide.
bestiary.tactics: You can't win — buy time, redirect it (noise elsewhere), or escape. Loud actions, lights, and fire add ticks.
bestiary.kill: — (you don't kill a horde; you survive it).
bestiary.notes: Treat a crowd as a single advancing threat with a Clock. See "The Horde (Mass Rules)".

bestiary.name: Looter / Scavenger
bestiary.drive: Take what you have and leave; survive.
bestiary.tactics: Will run from a fair fight but is deadly cornered or in numbers. Morale breaks at half strength — give them an out and they take it.
bestiary.kill: They die like anyone — and reanimate like anyone.
bestiary.notes: Desperate, armed, jumpy. Often more scared than you are.

bestiary.name: Desperate Holdout
bestiary.drive: Defend this house, these people, this stockpile.
bestiary.tactics: Shoots first from cover, knows every angle of their place. High Composure at home; talk fast or don't approach.
bestiary.kill: Mortal. Their stockpile is the prize — and the reason they'll die over it.
bestiary.notes: Barricaded and twitchy. Not evil — terrified, and out of trust.

bestiary.name: National Guardsman
bestiary.drive: Hold the cordon. Follow orders — even ugly ones.
bestiary.tactics: Disciplined, armored, well-armed, rarely alone. Won't break as an individual; the unit can. Under Operation Ashfall, orders turn lethal.
bestiary.kill: Body armor (Soak 3, torso) makes the chest a bad target — go for the unprotected, or don't fight at all.
bestiary.notes: The safe zone's threat in a uniform. Most are just scared kids with rifles.

bestiary.name: Guard Fireteam (4)
bestiary.stat: Run as a Clock + a leader (use Guardsman stats)
bestiary.drive: Detain, turn back, or sanitize — per orders.
bestiary.tactics: Coordinated fire and movement; they flank and suppress. Treat as a 4–6 step Clock that fills as they close/maneuver, plus the leader as an individual. PCs win by avoidance, leverage, or talking — not a firefight.
bestiary.kill: A stand-up fight with a trained, armored fireteam is a death sentence. Use terrain, the dead, and words.
bestiary.notes: When the cordon decides you're a problem.

bestiary.name: Rick's People / Ranch Hands
bestiary.drive: Protect their own; build a life behind the walls.
bestiary.tactics: Armed and watchful but human — they can be reasoned with, traded with, earned. Every newcomer is a math problem to them.
bestiary.kill: Mortal, and they'd rather you weren't. Defend their ground hard if pushed.
bestiary.notes: Freedom on a knife's edge. Allies, rivals, or wardens depending on how you arrive.

bestiary.name: Feral Survivor
bestiary.drive: Weeks alone has rewired them — survive, by instinct.
bestiary.tactics: May not speak; reacts, doesn't reason. Treats you as either rescue or rival on a hair-trigger. Composure is shot — easily Broken, into flight or violence.
bestiary.kill: Tragic and mortal. Often more victim than monster.
bestiary.notes: What too long in the cold and the quiet does to a person.

tables.name: Scavenging (d6)
tables.desc: Roll on a success; the GM picks the local flavor.
entries: Survival score — cold gear, a sleeping bag, real boots, hand-warmers.
Fuel — a partial can, or a car worth siphoning.
Medical — bandages, antibiotics, painkillers (or animal equivalents).
Tools / weapon — multi-tool, crowbar, a blade, a few rounds of ammo.
Sustenance — food, water, a working lighter, batteries, a hand-crank radio.
Complication with a prize — the good stuff and a guarded survivor, a Cold-Stiff cluster, or a noise that ticks a clock.

tables.name: Random Roadside Encounters (d6)
tables.desc: On the move along the I-17 corridor.
entries: A car with the doors open and the family still belted in, beginning to stir (Trapped).
Survivors flagging you down — desperate, or bait.
A military checkpoint turning people back at gunpoint.
A lone Waker sprinting flat-out down the centerline toward your headlights.
Fire on the horizon and the question of whether to go toward the warmth.
Stillness — and the slow realization of why it's so quiet here.

tables.name: What's in the Car (d6)
tables.desc: For each likely vehicle the party searches.
entries: Useful and clean — phone charger, blanket, half a tank, snacks in the console.
Cold-weather gear in the trunk (someone packed for the mountains).
Locked, alarmed, or wheel-clubbed — noise risk to get in.
Nearly dry tank, but a full spare can / a tow strap / jumper cables.
Grim contents — the previous owner is still here, and stirring.
A small windfall and a witness: a hidden firearm, cash, meds — and someone watching from a window.

tables.name: Vehicle Trouble (d6)
tables.desc: When a Critical Failure or hard driving demands it.
entries: Stuck — snow, mud, or a ditch. A Brawn/Agility scene to free it (and noise while you do).
Out of fuel sooner than the gauge said. Siphon or walk.
It won't start — battery or cold. Time, tools, or a push-start.
Flat or blown tire on debris; the spare may be missing.
Overheating / warning light — baby it or it dies somewhere worse.
The road is the problem — a jam, a collapse, the cordon. Reroute on foot or off-road.

tables.name: Cold Complications (d6)
tables.desc: When the weather turns the screw.
entries: Whiteout — visibility to arm's length; everything is at least one step harder.
Wet through (sweat, slush, a fall) — your cold ladder advances faster until you dry out.
The fire/heater is the trap — it draws Cold-Stiffs back to life nearby.
Fuel for warmth runs low; a choice between heat now and travel later.
A companion is failing faster than the rest (old, young, hurt, soaked).
Ice — a fall, a dropped tool, a vehicle that won't grip. Fine-motor tasks at Desperate.

tables.name: A Loud Noise Just Happened (d6)
tables.desc: Gunshot, scream, crash, alarm. Roll for what answers.
entries: Nothing — yet. But the GM ticks the nearest clock and remembers.
A few heads turn your way out of the dark and start drifting in.
Something fast answers — a Waker, sprinting.
A Screamer takes up the cry and carries it; the horde clock jumps.
Living ears heard it too — survivors, looters, or the Guard come to look.
The big one: the nearest Horde clock fills a step and the tide turns toward you.

tables.name: How the Living React (d6)
tables.desc: First read on a stranger — shift up for a good Charm check, down for threats.
entries: Hostile — sees you as a threat or a resource. Weapons up.
Afraid — wants you gone; will fight if cornered.
Wary trade — keeps distance, talks through a door, deals carefully.
Desperate ask — needs something (meds, fuel, a gun, an escort) and will owe you.
Cautiously helpful — shares intel or shelter for now.
Genuine — an ally, or someone who reminds you what you're trying to stay.

tables.name: Lasting Injury (d8)
tables.desc: Roll when a character is stabilized from Down, or on a brutal blow. Severity (Minor/Major/Grievous) by how bad it was.
entries: Concussion / ringing skull — −1 to −4 Wits and perception until it clears.
Bad leg — limp; movement and Agility suffer; running is a check.
Wrecked arm/hand — that side is weak or useless; two-handed tasks are hard.
Cracked ribs — every exertion and deep breath hurts; −1 to −4 Brawn.
Deep gash — bleeds if pushed; reopens on hard activity without care.
Burns — painful, slow to heal, distracting; −1 to −4 to fine work.
Frostbite — lost feeling or fingers/toes; fine-motor tasks one or more steps harder.
Shock / the thousand-yard stare — Composure damage that won't recover until real safety.

tables.name: Critical Failure Mishap (d6)
tables.desc: On a natural 18, something goes wrong beyond the miss.
entries: Loud — a clatter, a misfire, a scream. Tick the nearest clock / roll on "A Loud Noise."
Broken — your tool, weapon, or gear snaps or jams (a gun needs a round to clear).
Exposed — you slip, drop something vital, or end up Prone / off-balance.
Overextended — a foe gets a free grab or bite; you're Grabbed.
Worse than you thought — the real situation is grimmer than it looked (more dead, thinner ice, an empty tank).
Cost to the body or nerve — take a point of VIT or Composure, GM's pick.

tables.name: Broken: Panic Response (d4)
tables.desc: At 0 Composure, until someone steadies you (Charm) or the danger ends.
entries: Freeze — Rooted, unable to act; you can only be moved or shielded.
Flight — you bolt, blindly, toward anything that feels like away.
Fixate — Tunnel Vision on one thing (a body, a door, a task) to the exclusion of all else.
Lash Out — you turn on whatever's nearest: a threat, a corpse, or — God help them — a friend.

tables.name: Found Ammo & Gear (d6)
entries: A handful of the wrong caliber — useful to trade, not to load.
A partial box that fits something you carry. Reload one weapon.
A better blade or a sturdy melee tool — quiet and reusable.
A working light + batteries, or a hand-crank/solar one.
A first-aid kit, or the makings of one.
The good find — a loaded firearm, a real coat, or a full fuel can. And probably a reason it was left.

tables.name: On the Radio (d6)
tables.desc: Static, snatches, and lies on the airwaves.
entries: Emergency Alert loop: remain indoors, do not approach others, do not render aid.
Deep Well Ranch broadcasting directions to the safe zone — calm, official, rationed.
Radio Rick: weather, road conditions, where the dead are thick, and his opinion of the Guard.
A family or group trying to reach each other across the static. You could answer.
Gunfire and shouting, then silence, then a carrier tone.
A rumor that won't die: a federal lab in Phoenix, and a cure. Probably a lie.

tables.name: Why It's So Quiet Here (d6)
tables.desc: When the party finds an unnervingly still place.
entries: Everyone already left — recently, in a hurry, leaving useful things behind.
Everyone already died — and hasn't gotten up yet, or is just out of sight.
The cold did it — a froze-over disaster; Cold-Stiffs everywhere, waiting for warmth.
Someone cleared it and is watching to see what you do.
A trap or a deadfall — the quiet is bait (looters, a holdout, a rigged building).
It's genuinely safe, for now — and that's its own kind of unsettling.

rewardStat.name: Grit
rewardStat.desc: 1 per surviving session; 3 = +1 attribute, 1 = a Knack.

costs: 3 Grit → raise one Attribute by +1 (post-creation cap 16).
1 Grit → learn a new Knack, or permanently raise max Composure by 1 (cap 6).
2 Grit → shake a lasting Injury for good, or steady a shattered nerve (clear a permanent Composure scar).

docs.npcStatFormat: Brawn / Agility / Wits / Charm | VIT | CMP | DEF

characterTemplate.note: Everyone begins in street clothes for a warm theme park — sneakers, hoodies, no cold-weather gear. Surviving Session 1 is partly a race to get warm.

reference.title: The Core Mechanic
reference.body:
When an outcome is uncertain and failure is interesting, roll **3d6** and try to roll **equal to or under** your relevant Attribute, adjusted by an **Ease Modifier**.

> **Success:** total ≤ (Attribute + Ease Modifier)
> **Failure:** total > (Attribute + Ease Modifier)

Because you roll three dice, results cluster around **10–11**. Wild swings are rare, so a competent character is *reliably* competent. This is deliberate: STRAIN Z rewards planning over gambling.

**Don't roll for everything.** If there's no real risk and no time pressure, just narrate success. Roll when a failure would cost something — time, blood, fuel, Composure, or safety.

reference.title: The Four Attributes
reference.body:
Everything keys off four attributes (start 8, range 3–16):

- **Brawn (BRN)** — melee, lifting, forcing, endurance, resisting cold. Sets **Vitality** (6 + Brawn).
- **Agility (AGL)** — speed, aim, balance, stealth, fine motor. Sets **Defense** (how hard you are to hit) and initiative.
- **Wits (WIT)** — perception, medicine, mechanics, nerve, quick thinking.
- **Charm (CHA)** — read people, persuade, lead, steady frightened friends.

Pick the attribute that fits the *fictional* approach, not the goal. Climbing a fence is **Brawn** if you haul yourself over, **Agility** if you go quick and quiet.

reference.title: Ease Modifiers (Difficulty)
reference.body:
The GM sets one Ease step based on the situation. It adjusts your target up or down:

| Ease | Mod | When |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple** | +2 | Favorable conditions, good tools, no pressure |
| **Average** | +0 | A fair, default challenge |
| **Hard** | −2 | Bad footing, poor light, improvised tools, real danger |
| **Desperate** | −4 | Long odds, terrible conditions, doing it mid-fight |
| **Heroic** | −6 | Should-be-impossible; only in extremis |

Set **one** Ease for the whole situation rather than stacking many small modifiers — it keeps play fast. If two big factors pull the same way, shift one extra step.

reference.title: Worked Example: A Single Check
reference.body:
*Leo has spiked a fever in the back of the minivan and Sarah is unraveling.* **Dana (Wits 11)** tries to keep them both calm and read the boy — that's a **Charm** check to steady Sarah... no: she's diagnosing, so it's **Wits**. Conditions are bad (cold, fear, a crying child), so the GM calls it **Hard (−2)**.

> Target = Wits 11 − 2 = **9**. Dana rolls **4 + 3 + 1 = 8**. 8 ≤ 9 → **success.**

She gets the read: this isn't an ordinary fever. Had she rolled a **3** (natural crit), she'd also notice the bite under his sleeve before anyone else. Had she rolled **18**, her own composure would crack at the worst moment.

reference.title: Probability Quick-Reference
reference.body:
Roughly, on 3d6 your chance to roll a target **or under**:

- **6 →** ~9% · **8 →** ~26% · **9 →** ~38% · **10 →** ~50% · **11 →** ~62% · **12 →** ~74% · **14 →** ~91%

So an average task (target ~10–11) is a coin-flip-plus; **Hard (−2)** turns an 11 into a 9 (~38%); **Desperate (−4)** into a 7 (~16%). Use the **Probability** tool in this Rules screen to see the full curve for any target.

reference.title: Criticals & Opposed Checks
reference.body:
- **Natural 3** = **Critical Success.** Best possible outcome, plus a bonus.
- **Natural 18** = **Critical Failure.** Something goes wrong beyond the miss — a jam, a snapped tool, a noise that draws the dead. (See the **Critical Failure Mishap** table.)

**Opposed Checks:** when two people clash (arm-wrestle, a chase, a battle of wills), both roll under their relevant Attribute. Whoever **succeeds by more** — the larger gap between roll and target — wins. Ties favor the defender / the status quo.

reference.title: Character Creation
reference.body:
1. **Attributes.** Start every attribute at **8**, then distribute **10 points** (max **14** at creation; the lifetime cap is **16**).
2. **Derived stats** fall out automatically: **VIT** = 6 + Brawn · **Composure** = 3 (+1 if Wits ≥ 12, +1 if Charm ≥ 12) · **Defense** from Agility.
3. **Background** gives a starting **Knack** (a thing you're good at) and a little gear.
4. **A Tie** (someone or something you're trying to get back to) and a **Fear** (what the apocalypse will make you face).
5. You begin in **street clothes for a warm theme park** — sneakers, hoodies, no cold gear. Surviving Session 1 is partly a race to get warm.

You are an ordinary person. There are no superheroes here — only people who plan well and get lucky.

reference.title: Knacks & Backgrounds
reference.body:
A **Knack** is a narrow competence — *Medicine & stabilizing the wounded*, *Engines & siphoning*, *Talking people down*, *Quiet movement*. When a Knack clearly applies, the GM shifts your Ease **one step better** (e.g., Hard → Average), or lets you simply succeed at routine uses.

Your **Background** sets your first Knack and starting gear. You gain more Knacks with **Grit** as you survive. Knacks are about *fiction first* — name what you're good at, and lean on it.

reference.title: Advancement (Grit)
reference.body:
Survival is the only teacher. Each character who **lives through a session** earns **1 Grit**. Spend it between sessions:

- **3 Grit →** raise one Attribute by **+1** (post-creation cap **16**).
- **1 Grit →** learn a new **Knack**, or permanently raise **max Composure** by 1 (cap 6).
- **2 Grit →** shake a lasting **Injury** for good, or heal a permanent Composure scar.

Grit is also a quiet clock on the campaign: the longer this goes, the harder and colder it gets, and Grit is what keeps you a step ahead of it.

reference.title: Order of a Combat Round
reference.body:
Combat is fast and dangerous. A few seconds of bad luck can end a person.

1. **Seize the Moment.** Each PC rolls an **Agility** check (Average). **Success** = you act in the **Fast Phase**, before the threats. **Failure** = **Slow Phase**, after. (Skip in a clear ambush — the ambusher goes first.)
2. Resolve **Fast-Phase actions**, then **enemy actions**, then **Slow-Phase actions**.
3. Repeat each round until it's over.

A character gets **one meaningful action** per round, plus a few words and a step.

> *Sam and Dana round a snowbank into three Shamblers. Sam rolls Agility and succeeds (Fast); Dana fails (Slow). Sam acts → the dead act → Dana acts. Sam uses his window to shove the nearest one into the others and break for the door rather than trade bites.*

reference.title: Defense & To-Hit (how getting hit works)
reference.body:
STRAIN Z is **symmetric**: every attack — yours or a threat's — is a 3d6 roll-under, and the **defender's Defense is the Ease** the attacker uses.

**Attacking something:** roll under your skill (Brawn for melee, Agility for ranged). The target's **Defense** sets your Ease; the GM shifts it **one step** for circumstances.

| Defender's state | Your Ease to hit |
|---|---|
| Slow/sluggish dead (Cold-Stiff), pinned, point-blank | **Simple (+2)** |
| Ordinary target (Shambler, a calm person) | **Average (+0)** |
| Quick or evasive (Waker, an agile, moving person) | **Hard (−2)** |
| Behind cover / in the dark / sprinting | **Desperate (−4)** |

A creature's bestiary line gives its Defense directly (a Waker is *Hard*, a Cold-Stiff is *Simple*).

**Getting attacked:** when a threat comes for **you**, it rolls 3d6 ≤ its **Attack rating**, with an **Ease penalty equal to your Defense**. Your Defense comes from **Agility** (see your sheet), improved by **cover, darkness, and movement**, and wrecked by being **Down, Prone, Grabbed, or surrounded** (often −2 or worse).

> *A Waker (Attack 11) lunges at **Sam**. Sam's Defense is +1 (Agility 10), and he's crouched behind a car door for +1 cover → Defense +2. The Waker rolls 3d6 ≤ 11 − 2 = **9**, gets a 12 → **miss**; the door holds. Next round Sam is caught in the open (Defense +1): the Waker rolls ≤ 10, gets a 7 → it has him. Roll the bite.*

**Mass threats are different:** a horde isn't a to-hit roll — it's a Clock you outrun or outsmart (see *The Horde*).

reference.title: Attacking, Damage & Soak
reference.body:
On a **hit**, roll the weapon's **damage** dice. Subtract the target's **Soak** (from armor/cover). The rest comes off **VIT** — or, for the dead, off **Body**.

> **Damage to VIT/Body = (weapon dice) − Soak.**

**Clean Hit:** beat the target number by **5 or more** → **+2 damage** (and you may stagger or knock them **Prone**).

> *Dana (Brawn 11) swings a **crowbar (1d6+1)** at a **Shambler** (Defense Average). Target = 11. She rolls 4+3+2 = **9** ≤ 11 → hit. Damage 1d6+1 = **5**; the Shambler has no Soak and Body 5 → its torso is wrecked and it drops to a **Crawler** (still a bite threat until the head is gone). Had she rolled a **6** (beat 11 by 5), the Clean Hit's +2 would have been enough to take the head.*

> *A **looter** in a **ballistic vest (Soak 3 torso)** takes a 2d6 = 8 pistol hit to the chest → 8 − 3 = **5** to VIT. A hit to his unarmored arm ignores the vest.*

Remember: **guns are loud.** Each shot ticks the nearest **Horde/Wakes clock** (1 for a pistol, more for a shotgun/chainsaw). And against the dead, damage alone doesn't *kill* — see **Headshots**.

reference.title: Range & Cover
reference.body:
Ranged weapons list a band (Short / Medium / Long). Shooting **past a weapon's comfortable band** worsens your Ease:

- **Short** (a room, a few cars): +0
- **Medium** (across a lot, a street): **−2 (Hard)**
- **Long** (a field, rooftop to rooftop): **−4 (Desperate)** — and ranged **headshots beyond short range take an extra −2**.

**Cover** improves a defender's Defense (light cover ~+1, solid cover like a car door or wall ~+2) and can stop shots outright if it's thick enough — treat as Soak or a flat block. Cover, darkness, smoke, and snow cut both ways: harder to be hit, harder to hit.

reference.title: Called Shots & Hit Locations
reference.body:
By default you strike center mass. **Declare a called shot** before rolling to target a location, at a penalty:

- **Head — Desperate (−4):** on a hit, **instantly kills a person** and **destroys a zombie** (the only true kill). Ranged headshots beyond Short range take an extra −2.
- **Limb / leg — Hard (−2):** enough damage (≥ the target's Body for the dead) **severs or cripples** it — drop a walker to a **Crawler**, lame a runner, end a chase.
- **Arm / weapon hand — Hard (−2):** disarm or disable that hand; knock a gun from a looter.

Against the dead, a **body** called shot does nothing special — they don't feel it. Spend your called shots on **heads and legs**.

reference.title: Headshots & Killing the Dead
reference.body:
A zombie **only truly dies when its brain is destroyed.** Body damage cripples but doesn't stop it — a "killed" walker with an intact head gets back up, or drags itself after you.

- **Headshot:** a called shot to the head — **Desperate (−4)**. On a hit, the zombie is **destroyed outright**, regardless of its Body. (Beyond Short range, an extra −2.)
- **Crippling instead:** damage ≥ a zombie's **Body** to the **legs** drops it to a **Crawler** — slow, low, far less dangerous, but still a bite threat **until the head is gone**.
- **A finishing blow** on a downed/pinned zombie (no roll needed if it can't resist) is the safe, quiet way to make sure — a knife through the eye.

This is the core tension: every "kill" is a question of whether you have the time, the angle, and the nerve to make it a *real* one.

reference.title: Wounds, Going Down & Injuries
reference.body:
- At **0 VIT** a character is **Down**: bleeding out, unconscious, or crippled. Without aid they die in **1d6 rounds** (in combat) or **1d6 × 10 minutes** (after).
- A **Medicine (Wits)** check — **Average** with a kit, **Hard** without — stabilizes them at **1 VIT** but leaves a lasting **Injury** (roll on the *Lasting Injury* table; severity by how bad the hit was).
- **Injuries** (Minor −1 / Major −2 / Grievous −4 to related actions) linger until proper rest and care (see **Healing & Recovery**).

> **The cardinal rule:** **whoever dies, by any cause, reanimates in 2–6 hours.** A downed ally is a future threat unless they recover — or unless someone makes a terrible choice with a knife and lives with it.

reference.title: Healing & Recovery
reference.body:
Nobody bounces back here — recovery is slow, and it competes with the cold and the clock.

- **Catching your breath** (a safe, warm hour with water and food): recover a small amount of **VIT** (about your **Brawn ÷ 3**, min 1) and **1 Composure**.
- **A full, warm night's rest** somewhere genuinely safe: recover up to **half your max VIT**, **1–2 Composure**, and reduce one **Injury** by a tier.
- **Medicine (Wits)** + supplies can speed this, stop bleeding, fight infection-from-wounds (not the *Bite*), and ease pain so an Injury penalty drops a step for a scene.
- **Real safety, warmth, food, and time** are the only things that bring people all the way back — which is exactly what the apocalypse keeps taking away.

There is **no medical fix for the Bite.** Antibiotics, painkillers, and clean bandages save you from dying of ordinary wounds — not from the strain.

reference.title: Ammo, Reloading & Noise
reference.body:
**Guns end fights — and start worse ones.** Track ammo loosely as a scarce resource (a handful of rounds, a partial box) rather than counting every bullet. Run dry on a **Critical Failure** or when the GM calls it dramatic.

- **Reloading** a magazine is your **action**; a revolver or shotgun reloads **round by round** (often across several turns).
- **Jams** happen on Critical Failures — clearing one costs a round (a revolver never jams; that's its whole appeal).
- **Noise** is the real cost: every shot ticks the nearest **Horde/Wakes clock** (pistol +1, rifle +1, shotgun/chainsaw +2). A suppressed crack, a bow, or a quiet blade keeps the dead from converging. The party that solves problems with a crowbar lives longer than the party that solves them with a Glock.

reference.title: Getting Grabbed & Breaking Free
reference.body:
When a dead hand closes on you, you're **Grabbed**: **−2 Defense**, you can't move, and a thing with teeth is now in range of your throat.

- **Break free:** **Desperate (−4) Brawn or Agility**. Success = you tear loose (and may go Prone). Failure = you're held, and it **bites** (roll its attack).
- A **friend** can help — pull you free, or behead the thing — usually easier than freeing yourself, but it costs *their* action.
- The lesson: don't let them get a grip. **Reach weapons** (spear), **doorways**, and **keeping your back clear** are how you avoid this. Once several have hold of you at once, it's the **Horde** rules — and a Desperate check just to live.

reference.title: The Bite & Infection
reference.body:
Everyone is **already a carrier.** That's the secret the world doesn't know yet.

- **Any death, by any cause → reanimation in 2–6 hours.** Cold, a fall, a heart attack, old age — all of it gets up.
- A **bite that breaks skin → you die in 1d6 days, then turn.** A fever, then the chills, then the end. **No cure exists.**
- A bite is the cruelest clock in the game: the character keeps playing, keeps helping, keeps saying goodbye over days.

> *The Paramedic is bitten on the Cordes hike sealing a door. He doesn't tell anyone for an hour. He's still the best medic the party has — for about five more days.*

Some try **amputation** in the first round or two after a limb bite. It's a maiming, desperate, often-fatal gamble the table can attempt — never a guarantee, always a horror.

reference.title: Cold & Exposure (the central system)
reference.body:
The snow is the quiet villain. Out in it, make an **hourly Brawn check**, the **difficulty worsening one step each consecutive exposed hour** (Average → Hard → Desperate…). Fail and you slide down the ladder; warmth halts and reverses it (recover ~one stage per 30 minutes warm).

| Stage | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Warm** | No penalty. |
| **Chilled** | −1 to all rolls. |
| **Shivering** | −2 to all rolls; fine-motor tasks one step harder. |
| **Frostnipped** | −4 to all rolls; hand tasks at Desperate; risk losing fingers/toes. |
| **Hypothermic** | Incapacitated, drifting under. Without warming, **die in ~1 hour — then turn.** |

> *The party reaches the Sunset Point rest area three hours cold. Marcus (Brawn 13) is still Warm; the off-duty nurse (Brawn 7) is already **Shivering** and dropping fast. The dead aren't the threat in this scene — the wind is, and the search for a working heater.*

**Warmth is a resource you spend** — fuel, a fire, an engine, a body — and every warm thing you make is also a beacon that can wake the Cold-Stiffs.

reference.title: Composure & Panic (the mind)
reference.body:
**Composure** is your nerve. Witnessing horror — a child turning, an impossible choice, raw gore — costs Composure (the GM calls 1+). At **0 you're Broken** until someone steadies you (a **Charm** check) or the danger passes.

When Broken, roll or choose (d4): **Freeze** (Rooted), **Flight** (you bolt), **Fixate** (Tunnel Vision), or **Lash Out** (you turn on whatever's nearest — including a friend).

> *Watching Leo come back, **Sarah** breaks: she Fixates on her son, deaf to the danger. It costs Dana her action and a Charm check to pull Sarah away before the small hands reach her.*

Recover Composure with **safety, warmth, rest, and a steadying word.** Protect the small human moments — they're what let people hold the line when it counts.

reference.title: Encumbrance & Carrying
reference.body:
You can't carry the whole store, and a heavy pack gets people killed when it's time to run.

- **Light:** what you can grab and go with — no penalty.
- **Geared up:** a full pack, a long gun, layers — fine, but you're slower and louder.
- **Burdened:** hauling more than you should (loot, fuel cans, a wounded friend): **−1 Agility (and Defense)**, you tire faster, and stealth is hard.
- **Overloaded:** you can barely move. Running from a horde isn't an option — **drop the bag or be caught.**

Use this to make scavenging a real choice: *what do we leave behind, and what's worth dying for?*

reference.title: Scavenging, Fuel & Vehicles
reference.body:
Everything runs out. Searching a location is usually a single **Wits or Agility** check (quietly, quickly) — on a success, roll the **Scavenging** table; on a failure, you find little and maybe make noise.

- **Fuel** is the campaign's lifeblood. **Siphoning** is an **Agility** check; a partial tank is a real prize. Vehicles are warmth, range, and a wall — and a way to get loudly, fatally stuck (see *Vehicle Trouble*).
- **Vehicles** trade speed and shelter for fuel and noise. The **ADOT snowplow** is the golden ticket: weight, chains, and a blade that shoves the dead and the wreckage aside.
- **Time spent searching is time exposed** — to the cold, to the clock, and to whoever else is hunting the same shelves.

reference.title: The Horde (mass rules)
reference.body:
Treat a crowd as **a single advancing threat with a Clock.** Each tick, the horde closes distance or breaches a barrier. **Loud actions, lights, and fire add ticks.** PCs can't *win* a fight against a horde — they **buy time, redirect it** (noise elsewhere), or **escape.**

When the Clock **fills**, the horde is *on* them: everyone in reach makes a **Desperate Agility** check to break free or starts taking bites.

> *Whiskey Row, the fire spreading: the GM sets a 6-segment Horde clock. The gunshot that saved Marcus ticks it to 3. The party has maybe two more loud mistakes before the street is impassable — so they go quiet, go high, and let the fire pull the tide the other way.*

**Hordes are a countdown, not an enemy to defeat.**

reference.title: Reading the Dead
reference.body:
The dead hunt mostly by **sound**, then by **sight** (movement, light), and barely by smell. Use it:

- **Quiet is life.** Soft shoes, closed doors, hand signals. A whispered plan beats a shouted one; a blade beats a gun.
- **Lure and redirect.** A thrown bottle, a wind-up toy, a car alarm across the lot — noise pulls them *there* so you can move *here*. This is how you beat a horde you can't fight.
- **Light discipline at night.** A flashlight is a target. The dark hides you too — but it hides Crawlers and the Cold-Stiffs you're about to warm up.
- **They don't tire, plan, or feel cold the way you do** — but the fresh **Wakers** remember doors, stairs, and how to run. Don't assume "slow."

reference.title: The Living: People as Threats
reference.body:
The dead are the pressure; **the living are the danger.** Frightened, armed people make the worst decisions, and there are far more of them than there are bullets.

- **Talk first.** A **Charm** check (and what you're offering or threatening) sets the tone — see *How the Living React*. People want safety, supplies, or someone to trust; give them a path that isn't a fight.
- **Morale.** Living groups aren't fearless. When they're hurt, outmatched, or lose their leader, the GM rolls **Composure/Charm** for the group: they **flee, surrender, or escalate.** Looters break early; holdouts and the Guard hold longer.
- **Cost.** Every fight with the living is loud, costs irreplaceable people and ammo, and **everyone who dies gets back up.** The smartest survivors avoid, outmaneuver, or out-talk other humans — and save the violence for when there's no door left.

reference.title: GM Cheat Sheet
reference.body:
**Resolve:** 3d6 ≤ Attribute + Ease. Nat 3 crit success · Nat 18 crit fail · beat by 5+ = Clean Hit (+2 dmg).
**Ease:** Simple +2 · Average +0 · Hard −2 · Desperate −4 · Heroic −6.
**To hit:** target's **Defense** is your Ease (Cold-Stiff Simple, Shambler Average, Waker Hard, covered Desperate); shift one step for circumstances.
**Damage:** weapon dice − **Soak** → off **VIT** (people) or **Body** (dead). **Headshot −4 = true kill.**
**Defense (PCs):** from Agility (≤6 −1 · 7–9 +0 · 10–12 +1 · 13–15 +2 · 16 +3); +cover/dark/moving, −Down/Prone/Grabbed/surrounded.
**Down:** 0 VIT → dies in 1d6 rounds; Medicine stabilizes at 1 VIT + an Injury.
**Ranges:** Short +0 · Medium −2 · Long −4 (ranged headshot beyond Short: extra −2).
**Cold:** hourly Brawn, one step harder each exposed hour: Warm → Chilled (−1) → Shivering (−2) → Frostnipped (−4) → Hypothermic.
**Infection:** any death → turns in 2–6 hrs · bite → die in 1d6 days then turn · no cure.
**Guns are loud:** +1/+2 to the nearest Horde clock per shot. **Hordes are Clocks, not HP.**
**Grit:** 1/session; 3 = +1 Attribute (cap 16); 1 = a Knack or +1 max Composure.

reference.title: Running a Deadly Game
reference.body:
STRAIN Z is **dread over gore.** Keep these front of mind:

- **The dead are NOT the main threat** — cold, fuel, panic, and other people are. The dead are the *pressure* that turns ordinary problems lethal. Put a clock on the cold and the fuel as often as on the dead.
- **Roll only when failure is interesting.** Narrate the routine; reach for dice when blood, time, fuel, Composure, or safety is on the line.
- **Use Clocks, not hit-point slogs.** A horde, a fire, a thawing cluster, a freezing companion, a cordon going up — make threats *advance* a step at a time and let players see the countdown.
- **Quiet beats earn loud ones.** Protect the small human moments — they make the shocks land.
- **Telegraph lethality.** Show, don't ambush: the breath fogging, the thing in the seatbelt twitching, the gun that will bring the street down on them. Let players make informed, terrible choices.
- **Content warnings are canon:** child death and reanimation, a pandemic framing over the real early days of 2020, despair and exposure deaths. Handle with care; never gratuitous.

armor.name: Heavy winter layers (coat, gloves, boots)
armor.notes: Your real lifeline — warmth first, padding second. Getting properly dressed is half of surviving Session 1.

armor.name: Leather jacket / thick denim
armor.notes: Turns a graze into a bruise and makes a bite one step harder to break skin (where it covers).

armor.name: Work / welding gloves & apron
armor.notes: Protection exactly where the dead grab and bite — hands and arms. Cheap and worth it.

armor.name: Sports / bike gear (hockey, motocross)
armor.notes: Hard shells that shrug off bites; bulky and squeaky.

armor.name: Improvised (magazines, phone books, duct tape)
armor.notes: Cheap, hot, bulky, and degrades with every hit. Better than nothing.

armor.name: Riot gear (looted PD)
armor.notes: The visored helmet can turn an incoming bite or strike to the head. Hard to hear in.

armor.name: Ballistic vest
armor.notes: Stops rounds and chest bites cold; neck, limbs, and face are still wide open.

armor.name: Plate carrier + helmet (military)
armor.notes: The most protection in the campaign — and the heaviest, hottest, hardest to move and hear in. Guard and Deep Well only.
